title: Conductivity of a Non-Galilean--Invariant Fermi Liquid: Exact Solution of the Kinetic Equation
authors: Tatia Kiliptari, Vladimir I. Yudson, Dmitrii L. Maslov
abstract: We obtain an exact expression for the conductivity of a disordered,   non-Galilean-invariant Fermi liquid by solving the kinetic equation with both screened Coulomb and $z=3$ Pomeranchuk critical interactions. While consistent with previous asymptotic results, our solution shows that electron-electron interactions enter the conductivity solely via the quasiparticle scattering time, $τ_\mathrm{ee}$. Accordingly, the crossovers between the collisionless and hydrodynamic regimes occur when $1/τ_\mathrm{ee}$ becomes comparable to the larger of the impurity scattering rate and the probe frequency, $Ω$. In addition, the exact solution yields the optical response in the hydrodynamic regime, $Ω\ll 1/τ_\mathrm{ee}$, which is inaccessible within perturbation theory. Near a $z=3$ Pomeranchuk quantum critical point, consistency between the kinetic-equation and Kubo approaches requires proper inclusion of mass renormalization within the Eliashberg approximation, which also ensures that the crossover between the collisionless and hydrodynamic regimes in the optical conductivity occurs at the Planckian scale $Ω\sim T$.
summary: This paper provides an exact solution for the conductivity of a non-Galilean-invariant Fermi liquid under various interaction models. It corrects previous misconceptions by demonstrating that electron-electron interactions influence transport only through the quasiparticle lifetime. The work also provides a way to access the optical response in the hydrodynamic regime, which is otherwise inaccessible via perturbation theory.
main_problem: The paper aims to find an exact expression for the conductivity of a non-Galilean-invariant Fermi liquid across various temperature and frequency regimes, specifically clarifying the role of electron-electron interactions in current relaxation.
main_result: The authors derive an exact solution showing that electron-electron interactions affect conductivity solely through the quasiparticle scattering time, rather than a separate current relaxation rate, and provide the optical response in the hydrodynamic regime.
method: The study solves the linearized semi-classical kinetic equation by mapping it onto an inhomogeneous Legendre-type differential equation and using the Eliashberg approximation for the Hertz-Millis model.
model_or_system: A 2D non-Galilean-invariant Dirac system subject to both screened Coulomb interactions (treated via RPA) and $z=3$ Pomeranchuk critical interactions (Hertz-Millis model) in the presence of impurity scattering.
key_observables: DC resistivity, optical conductivity (real part), and quasiparticle scattering rate.
important_parameters: Frequency ($\Omega$), temperature ($T$), impurity scattering rate ($\gamma_i$), quasiparticle scattering time ($	au_{ee}$), and the Planckian scale ($\Omega \sim T$).
main_assumptions: Neglect of exchange processes in the Coulomb model, neglect of mass renormalization in the Coulomb model, and the use of the Eliashberg approximation (local self-energy and small vertex corrections).
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the kinetic equation and the physical models (Coulomb and Hertz-Millis), proceeds to the mathematical mapping onto a Legendre-type equation, derives the exact scaling form for conductivity, and concludes with an analysis of specific regimes (collisionless vs. hydrodynamic) and the role of mass renormalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) ee contribution to the dc conductivity of a disordered FL, σee(0, T), as a function of γT τi in units of e2 and for Ω2 pτi/εF = 100. Main panel: exact solution, Eq. (2b). Top and bottom insets: high- and low-temperature limits, respectively. (b) Optical conductivity Re σee(Ω, T) of a disorder-free FL as a function of Ω/γT , in units of e2 and for T = 0.1Ωp = 0.01εF. . In both panels, dotted (blue) and dashed (red) curves correspond to analytical results.
